name: code-analysis
description: Check if code is readable by non-developers - clear names, plain English comments, no jargon
capabilities: Detects cryptic variable and function names that need clarification
Flags unexplained technical jargon in code comments
Verifies What/Why/How documentation for functions and classes
Scores code readability from 0-100 with detailed issues
Supports Python, JavaScript, TypeScript, Java, Go, Ruby, and PHP
author: abereyes
securitySummary: This is a legitimate code readability analyzer with no security concerns. All 44 static findings are FALSE POSITIVES. The 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' patterns are misidentified regex patterns for code analysis (analyzing variable names like 'usr_tkn', 'tmp', 'idx'). The 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' patterns are documentation code fences (markdown syntax), not actual shell commands. The 'C2 keywords' finding references 'webhook' as a technical term in a jargon list, not command-and-control infrastructure. The skill only reads user-specified files via command-line arguments, uses standard Python libraries (argparse, os, re, json, pathlib), and makes zero network requests. Capabilities match the stated purpose: analyzing code readability for non-developers.
valueStatement: Code often contains cryptic names and technical jargon that non-developers cannot understand. This skill analyzes your code and scores how accessible it is to managers, stakeholders, and new team members.
limitations: Only analyzes Python, JavaScript, TypeScript, Java, Go, Ruby, and PHP files
May produce false positives in documentation-heavy files
Does not modify code - only reports issues

useCases.title: Audit team codebases
useCases.description: Review code readability scores across your codebase to identify documentation gaps and unclear naming patterns.

useCases.title: Onboard new team members
useCases.description: Ensure code is accessible to new hires who may not have deep programming experience.

useCases.title: Review stakeholder code
useCases.description: Check configuration scripts and automation code for clarity before sharing with non-technical stakeholders.

promptTemplates.title: Basic check
promptTemplates.prompt: Use the code-analysis skill to check readability of my_file.py

promptTemplates.title: Strict analysis
promptTemplates.prompt: Analyze my_script.ts with strict mode to find all naming and documentation issues

promptTemplates.title: Specific focus
promptTemplates.prompt: Check only the variable naming in config.rb using the code-analysis skill

promptTemplates.title: Full report
promptTemplates.prompt: Run a full readability audit on my project directory and output JSON results

bestPractices: Run the analyzer before sharing code with non-technical stakeholders
Use lenient mode when first addressing readability to avoid overwhelming output
Address cryptic naming issues first as they affect comprehension most
antiPatterns: Ignoring low scores without reviewing specific issues
Using the tool on generated code or boilerplate without context
Focusing only on score rather than understanding each issue type

faq.question: Which programming languages are supported?
faq.answer: Python, JavaScript, TypeScript, Java, Go, Ruby, and PHP files are supported.

faq.question: What readability score is considered good?
faq.answer: Scores above 75 indicate good readability. Above 90 is excellent and accessible to most non-developers.

faq.question: Can I integrate this with CI/CD pipelines?
faq.answer: Yes, use the --format json flag to get machine-readable output for automated pipelines.

faq.question: Is my code data sent anywhere?
faq.answer: No. The analyzer runs locally and makes no network requests. Your code never leaves your machine.

faq.question: Why are legitimate abbreviations flagged?
faq.answer: The tool flags abbreviations like usr or tkn that non-developers cannot understand. Standard terms like num or str are allowed.

faq.question: How is this different from linters?
faq.answer: Linters enforce syntax and style rules. This tool focuses on human comprehension - whether non-developers can understand the code logic and purpose.
